symbols: altar with slain bulls — the sacrificial contest between the Lord and Baal
kneeling figures around the altar — the defeated prophets of Baal or the repentant Israelites
Elijah standing apart in robes — the solitary prophet of God against the many
rocky mountain setting — Mount Carmel, the site of the divine contest
fire (implied) — God's answer by fire, proving His sovereignty
description: A panoramic scene set against a rocky mountainous landscape. In the center, several bare-backed men kneel around a stone altar bearing slain animals. To the right, an elderly bearded figure in ornate striped robes stands watching the proceedings, accompanied by other observers. On the left, additional figures watch from among rocks. The mood is tense and expectant, with the kneeling figures appearing exhausted or in supplication around the sacrificial offering.
theological_description: The contest on Mount Carmel is the definitive Old Testament confrontation between the worship of the true God and idolatry. Elijah's challenge — 'How long will you go limping between two different opinions? If the LORD is God, follow him; but if Baal, then follow him' (1KGS.18.21) — frames the scene as a moment of covenantal decision. The prophets of Baal have exhausted themselves in frenzied worship to a god who does not answer, illustrating the futility of idolatry (PSA.115.4-8). Elijah's calm confidence in the God who answers by fire prefigures Christ's own authority over the elements and His claim to exclusive worship. The sacrifice consumed by divine fire typologically anticipates the once-for-all sacrifice of Christ (HEB.10.12), and Elijah himself serves as a forerunner of John the Baptist (MAL.4.5, MAT.11.14), preparing the way for the Lord's decisive revelation.

scripture_refs.reason: The direct narrative of Elijah's sacrifice on Mount Carmel and the contest with the prophets of Baal
Elijah's challenge to Israel to choose between the Lord and Baal
The futility of idols that cannot see, hear, or act — illustrated by Baal's silence
The promise of Elijah's return before the great day of the Lord
The single sacrifice consumed by God prefigures Christ's once-for-all offering
